text: The video depicts a robotic-assisted right upper lobectomy for non-small cell lung cancer. The adenocarcinoma is located in the right upper lobe, with lymph node metastasis at station R-IV. The procedure utilizes a four-arm approach with a three-centimeter utility incision, performed without CO₂ insufflation.

The surgeon checks the fissure and hilum, confirming surgical feasibility. The pulmonary vein is dissected, sutured, and resected using a 30mm vascular stapler introduced through the posterior axillary port. The first pulmonary artery branch is dissected and divided using the same cartridge and port. The posterior segmental artery to the right upper lobe is exposed and divided after removing the N-I interlobar lymph nodes.

The surgeon dissects and encircles the bronchus for the upper lobe, dividing it with a 30mm robotic stapler. Subsequently, the fissure is divided using a 45mm robotic stapler. The specimen is then placed in an endo-bag and removed through the utility incision.

The surgeon performs lymphadenectomy at stations R-II and R-IV, dividing the azygos vein to enhance exposure. This maneuver provides a clear view between the trachea and the superior vena cava, where the lymph nodes are visibly attached to the surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs a subcarinal lymphadenectomy, carefully dissecting and removing lymphatic tissue from the subcarinal space.
The patient didn't have any postoperative complications and the chest tube was removed in postoperative day three.
